benchmark: OmniFuse vs synaptic-memory — extra download-only BEIR/MTEB datasets
note: synaptic ships these only as download scripts (not committed data); fetched from HuggingFace via synaptic tests/benchmark/download_datasets.py. Lexical track: omni field-weighted BM25 vs synaptic FTS-only (run_public_dataset), MRR@10.

datasets.name: FiQA
datasets.note: synaptic FTS not measured: 57,638-doc SQLite ingest too slow on this 16GB box

datasets.name: MultiLongDoc-ko
datasets.note: 193MB of long docs; omni in-memory index capped doc text to 6000 chars (zero-infra RAM bound), synaptic FTS not measured

conclusion: On generic flat-text BEIR/MTEB IR (no titles, no citation graph), OmniFuse and synaptic are at BM25-family parity: 2 wins each of the 4 head-to-heads measured, all within +/-0.02 MRR. OmniFuse's decisive wins are on STRUCTURED corpora (finreg citation graph, titled document sets) where field-weighted BM25 + graph-companion fusion have something to exploit; on unstructured passage IR neither system dominates.
honest_limitations: synaptic's private corpora (krra/assort/x2bee) are gitignored — not in its repo, cannot be run.
FiQA/MultiLongDoc synaptic-FTS not measured (ingest time / RAM on a 16GB shared box).
MultiLongDoc omni used a 6000-char doc cap — omnifuse's zero-infra in-memory BM25 cannot hold 193MB of long docs on 16GB RAM; a disk backend (Fuseki) would be needed for full text.
